text: he walked, one sound caught his attention over the rest. He’d noticed it before, in this part of town, but had never investigated. A high-pitched, clanging tinkle; little bits of metal tapping together, perhaps . The first of the human shacks entered his view as the path shifted gently northwest, leading away from the fenced-in factory. Ronin glanced up briefly at the cracked remains of the roadway that once bridged the tracks. The north end would have led directly into the Bot District, long ago. Most of the rubble had been scavenged; only the most irregular chunks of concrete and rebar remained piled beneath the crumbled ramps . The sound grew more distinct as he neared the human dwellings. He couldn’t help but compare the shacks to the wall on the other side of the road. The resemblance was undeniable, when he broke them into their components. Both were hobbled-together declarations of defiance in a homicidal world, imperfect but somehow practical . He didn’t understand the logic chains his processors followed. No, he couldn’t consider them logic chains; there was little logical thought involved. Observations and baseless speculations. Nothing that did him any good . Yet wasn’t it that manner of thinking that allowed him to find what other dustwalkers had missed out in the wasteland ? A louder, higher clang claimed Ronin’s attention. He turned his head to the left. One of the shacks stood at the edge of the dirt-and-gravel pathway, and from its eaves dangled a metal loop with an eclectic array of items attached to it . He changed his course, walking to the shack. It was a simple device; the loop hung on a single piece of fishing line, and more lines of varying lengths suspended the forks, spoons, knives, and keys from the bottom of the ring. The objects tapped into each other in the wind, producing sounds of varying tones and pitches . Why would a human create such a thing? Did they find the noises it made appealing ? Another sound drifted to his receptors, this one from a living throat, wordless but distinct. Humming. A female human hummed with the chiming, matching its pace but not its notes, complimenting it without mimicking . Tilting his head to the side, Ronin approached the entry. A gap had been left between the door and the jamb, granting him view of the figure moving inside . Chapter Three T he woman’s skirts swept around her legs as she spun with a grace Ronin doubted he could replicate. Her entire body moved as one, though the individual parts belied each other’s motions. It was a contradiction — subtle but powerful, wild and yet restrained . Though incomplete, his memory retained a surprising amount of information on humans — including details of their anatomy; the muscles, tissue, and skeletons that enabled their movement. Paired with his knowledge of basic physics, he should have understood why she was able to move like that . He’d seen other humans dance, in other settlements, but he’d never seen anyone move the way she was now. Knowledge of the underlying physical systems and unseen natural forces at work did nothing to explain how she was capable of such unpredictable, mesmerizing movement . This woman presented a new mystery, a new puzzle — human grace. Her dance lacked precision, but it was made up for by a raw, powerful quality Ronin could not adequately define . He studied her face as she turned again. Her eyes were closed, her pink lips turned down in a frown. Loose strands of her damp, red hair hung in her face, brushing over her freckle-dusted cheeks. Her lower lip quivered infinitesimally, coinciding with a brief break in her humming . She hesitated for a fraction of a second before resuming her dance . Ronin stepped closer to the door, leaning in until he was nearly touching it. The wind picked up, the gentle tinkling of the chimes interrupted by harsh clanging as several of the pieces hit his rucksack . The woman froze, pale blue eyes locked on Ronin . They stared at each other for six seconds before she finally spoke. “Who are you ?” Placing a hand on the edge of the door, Ronin slid it aside, and lifted a foot across the threshold . Her eyes flickered to his fingers, going wide, and she leapt back. With a fumbling hand, she reached behind her and picked up a steel bar. “ Get out !” “You asked who I am,” he said, pausing midstride in the doorway, booted foot hanging in the air . “I know what you are.” She raised her weapon with both hands, knuckles white. “You’re a bot. Get out !” “Haven’t come in yet .” Possibilities spiraled through his CPU, attributed arbitrary probabilities as they passed. A bot could guess what a human might do, but it was impossible to develop a definitive answer. Regardless, she’d only manage — at worst — a single swing. He was already due for repairs. One more dent wouldn’t change that . “You’re not welcome here, bolt bucket .” “You asked who I am, not what .” “I don’t care who you are .” “Then why bother asking at all ?” Her feet were planted wide apart, her stance defensive. A faint tremor ran through her arms as she adjusted her grip on her improvised weapon . The muscles of her jaw clenched and relaxed. “Because I thought you were human .” Pink blossomed on her cheeks, and she broke eye contact for a moment. Her weapon sagged. Fear, fatigue, or resignation ? “Would you dance again?” he asked . When she looked up at him again, her expression had changed, lips pressed into a tight line and brows low. “You’re shitting me, right? No. You want dancing, go to Kitty’s. I don’t fucking do that anymore .” Kitty’s. He knew of it, but had never gone inside. Had he missed the opportunity
